messages.content: isn't it wild how a coffee that was like 2 bucks when i was a teenager is basically 6 now

Yeah, it really is striking when you think about it that way! There's something about a specific everyday purchase like coffee that makes inflation feel really *real* and personal, compared to just hearing abstract percentages in the news.

Do you remember roughly when you were a teenager? Because the timing matters a lot - if that was like 20-30 years ago, some of that price increase is just normal inflation over time. But honestly coffee has also gotten more expensive *faster* than general inflation in recent years, especially with supply chain issues and bad harvests in coffee-growing regions.

And then there's also the whole specialty coffee culture shift - like even a basic drip coffee at a lot of places has kind of gotten pulled upmarket in how it's priced.

Does it change your habits at all, or do you just kind of absorb the cost?
i was telling my coworker, feels like everything just quietly crept up and we all kinda accepted it

That's such a good observation - "quietly crept up" is exactly the right way to describe it. It's almost like it happens gradually enough that there's no single moment where you go "okay THIS is too much" and push back. You just... adjust.

And there's probably something psychological going on too, like once a price is established it becomes the new normal pretty fast. Nobody's out here refusing to buy groceries, so what choice do you really have?

It's interesting that you were talking about it with your coworker though - do you find that's a pretty common topic lately? Because I feel like more people are having that conversation out loud now, kind of comparing notes on what they're noticing.

There's also something a little weird about how it's so universal but also feels kind of isolating? Like everyone's dealing with it but it still feels like a personal squeeze on YOUR budget specifically.

Are you and your coworker in a similar boat with it, or do people around you seem to be handling it differently?
makes me a little nostalgic for the days when a 20 in your pocket felt like real money lol

Ha, right?! A $20 used to feel like you were set for the day - grab food, do something, maybe still have change left over. Now it's like... that's basically one decent meal and a coffee and you're done.

There's something kind of bittersweet about that nostalgia though. Like you're not just missing the prices, you're probably missing that feeling of things feeling a bit more *manageable* and carefree? When you're a teenager $20 feels like freedom almost.

Now you hand over a $20 and you're already mentally calculating what you gave up to spend it. That mental load of constantly doing the math is kind of exhausting in itself.

Do you find yourself thinking about money differently than you used to, or has it always been something you've been pretty conscious of?
anyway not complaining really, just one of those shower thoughts about how prices change over time

Haha fair enough, sometimes you just notice something and it's worth a "huh, wild" moment without it being a whole thing. Those shower thoughts are some of the best ones honestly - just your brain making connections while you're going about your day.

And hey it's lunchtime on a weekday, you've got coffee on the brain - makes total sense that's where your head went! 😄

What else is going on with your day?
